questions.stem: A topological space $X$ is Hausdorff (T2) when which condition holds?
options: Any two distinct points have disjoint open neighborhoods.
Every convergent sequence is eventually constant.
questions.explanation:
The Hausdorff (T2) axiom requires that distinct points $x 
e y$ admit disjoint open sets $U 
i x$ and $V 
i y$. Points being closed is the weaker T1 axiom, and singletons being open describes the discrete topology.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: A space $X$ is T1 if and only if which condition holds?
options: Every finite subset (in particular every singleton) is closed.
Every two points have disjoint neighborhoods.
questions.explanation: The T1 axiom is equivalent to all singletons being closed (hence all finite sets closed). It says: for distinct $x, y$, there is an open set containing $x$ but not $y$. It is weaker than Hausdorff.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: The T0 (Kolmogorov) axiom states which of the following for distinct points $x \ne y$?
options: Both have neighborhoods excluding the other.
At least one of them has an open neighborhood not containing the other.
questions.explanation: T0 requires that for any two distinct points, at least one has an open neighborhood missing the other (the points are topologically distinguishable). This is the weakest of the standard separation axioms.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: In a Hausdorff space, a convergent sequence (or net) has which property regarding its limit?
options: It need not converge at all.
It can converge to several points.
Its limit must be an isolated point.
questions.explanation: In a Hausdorff space limits are unique: if a sequence converged to two distinct points, disjoint neighborhoods of those points would each eventually contain the tail, which is impossible. Uniqueness of limits is one of the main reasons Hausdorff spaces are convenient.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: A space is regular (the separation part of T3) when which separation is possible?
options: Any two points can be separated by a continuous function.
A point and a closed set not containing it can be separated by disjoint open sets.
Any two closed sets can be separated by disjoint open sets.
Any two open sets are disjoint.
questions.explanation: Regularity requires that a point $x$ and a closed set $C$ with $x \notin C$ can be enclosed in disjoint open sets. Combined with T1 this gives the T3 axiom. Separating two closed sets is the stronger normality condition.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: A space is normal (the separation part of T4) when which separation is possible?
options: Any two disjoint closed sets can be separated by disjoint open sets.
Any two points can be separated by disjoint open sets.
A point and a closed set can be separated.
Every open cover has a finite subcover.
questions.explanation: Normality requires that any two disjoint closed sets $A, B$ can be enclosed in disjoint open sets $U \supseteq A$, $V \supseteq B$. Together with T1 this is the T4 axiom. It is the hypothesis of the Urysohn lemma.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: The Urysohn lemma states that in a normal space, two disjoint closed sets $A$ and $B$ can be separated by which object?
options: A single open set containing both.
A compact set lying between them.
A continuous function $f : X \to [0, 1]$ with $f = 0$ on $A$ and $f = 1$ on $B$.
questions.explanation: The Urysohn lemma guarantees, for disjoint closed sets $A, B$ in a normal space, a continuous function $f : X \to [0,1]$ that is $0$ on $A$ and $1$ on $B$. This converts the set-separation of normality into separation by a real-valued function.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: Every metric space, with its metric topology, satisfies which separation property?
options: It is T0 but not always T1.
questions.explanation: Metric spaces are Hausdorff: distinct points $x \ne y$ at distance $d = d(x,y) > 0$ have disjoint balls of radius $d/2$. In fact every metric space is normal (T4), so all the standard separation axioms hold.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: Which chain of implications among the separation axioms is correct?
options: T4 implies T2 with no further conditions assumed.
questions.explanation: The standard hierarchy reads T2 (Hausdorff) implies T1 implies T0; each axiom is stronger than the next. (For the higher axioms, T3 and T4 are usually defined to include T1, which then gives T4 implies T3 implies T2; but T4 alone as just normality need not imply T2.)
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: Is the cofinite topology on an infinite set $X$ a T1 space?
options: Yes; singletons are closed because their complements are cofinite, hence open.
Yes, and it is also Hausdorff.
No, it is not even T0.
questions.explanation: In the cofinite topology a singleton $\{x\}$ has complement $X \setminus \{x\}$, which is cofinite and therefore open; so $\{x\}$ is closed. Hence the space is T1. (It is not Hausdorff, as shown separately.)
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: Is the cofinite topology on an infinite set $X$ Hausdorff?
options: Yes, by the same argument that makes it T1.
Yes, but only if $X$ is uncountable.
It depends on the cardinality of $X$.
No; any two nonempty open sets intersect, so disjoint neighborhoods do not exist.
questions.explanation: In the cofinite topology on an infinite set, every nonempty open set is cofinite, so any two nonempty open sets meet (their complements are finite, their intersection cofinite). Thus distinct points cannot have disjoint neighborhoods, and the space is not Hausdorff.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: Is the indiscrete topology $\{\emptyset, X\}$ on a set with at least two points a T0 space?
options: No; the only open sets are $\emptyset$ and $X$, so no point can be separated from another.
It is T0 but not T1.
questions.explanation: In the indiscrete topology the only open sets are $\emptyset$ and $X$, so the only open set containing any point is $X$, which contains every other point too. No two distinct points are topologically distinguishable, so the space is not even T0.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: Is the discrete topology on any set Hausdorff?
options: No, only finite discrete spaces are Hausdorff.
Yes; distinct points $x, y$ have the disjoint open singletons $\{x\}$ and $\{y\}$.
Only if the set is finite.
questions.explanation: In the discrete topology every singleton is open, so distinct points $x \ne y$ are separated by the disjoint open sets $\{x\}$ and $\{y\}$. Hence every discrete space is Hausdorff (indeed normal).
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: Every compact Hausdorff space has which of the following stronger properties?
options: It is metrizable in all cases.
questions.explanation: A compact Hausdorff space is normal. Disjoint closed subsets of a compact Hausdorff space are compact, and disjoint compact sets in a Hausdorff space can be separated by disjoint open sets. Compact Hausdorff spaces need not be discrete, connected, or metrizable.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: A subspace of a Hausdorff space is which of the following?
options: Hausdorff only if it is closed.
Hausdorff only if it is open.
questions.explanation: The Hausdorff property is hereditary: any subspace of a Hausdorff space is Hausdorff, since disjoint open separating sets in the whole space intersect the subspace in disjoint relatively open separating sets. No closedness or openness is needed.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: A finite Hausdorff space must have which topology?
options: A topology with exactly two open sets.
questions.explanation: In a finite T1 (hence Hausdorff) space every singleton is closed, so every subset (a finite union of singletons) is closed, making every subset open. Thus a finite Hausdorff space is discrete.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: The product of two Hausdorff spaces, with the product topology, is which of the following?
options: Hausdorff only if both are compact.
Hausdorff only if one factor is discrete.
questions.explanation: A product of Hausdorff spaces is Hausdorff. If $(x_1, x_2) \ne (y_1, y_2)$ they differ in some coordinate, say the first; disjoint neighborhoods $U, V$ of $x_1, y_1$ give disjoint basic open sets $U \times Y_2$ and $V \times Y_2$ separating the points.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: Which implication correctly relates the higher separation axioms (with T1 assumed in T3 and T4)?
options: T3 and T4 are unrelated to T2.
questions.explanation: Under the convention that T3 means regular plus T1 and T4 means normal plus T1, one has T4 implies T3 implies T2 implies T1 implies T0. Normal-and-T1 spaces are regular-and-T1, which are Hausdorff.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: The Hausdorff condition on a space $X$ is equivalent to which property of the diagonal $\Delta = \{(x, x) : x \in X\}$ in $X \times X$?
options: The diagonal is closed in $X \times X$.
questions.explanation:
$X$ is Hausdorff if and only if the diagonal $\Delta$ is closed in $X 	imes X$. A point $(x,y)$ off the diagonal has $x 
e y$, and disjoint neighborhoods $U 
i x$, $V 
i y$ give a basic open set $U 	imes V$ around $(x,y)$ missing $\Delta$.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: Is the Sierpinski space $X = \{0, 1\}$ with topology $\{\emptyset, \{1\}, X\}$ a T0 space? Is it T1?
options: It is neither T0 nor T1.
It is both T0 and T1.
It is T0 but not T1.
It is T1 but not T0.
questions.explanation: The open set $\{1\}$ contains $1$ but not $0$, so the two points are topologically distinguishable and the space is T0. But there is no open set containing $0$ and excluding $1$ (the only open set with $0$ is $X$), so $\{0\}$ is not closed and the space is not T1.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: The Urysohn metrization theorem gives a sufficient condition for a space to be metrizable. Which spaces does it apply to?
options: Every regular space that is also second countable (has a countable basis).
questions.explanation: The Urysohn metrization theorem states that a regular space with a countable basis (second countable) is metrizable. Such a space embeds in the countable product $[0,1]^{\mathbb{N}}$, which is metrizable.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: Which of the following spaces is T1 but not Hausdorff?
options: An infinite set with the cofinite topology.
The indiscrete topology on two points.
questions.explanation: An infinite cofinite-topology space is T1 (singletons are closed) but not Hausdorff (nonempty open sets always intersect). Metric and discrete spaces are Hausdorff, and the indiscrete two-point space is not even T0.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: In a T1 space, what is the relationship between a point $x$ and the limit points of a set?
options: Every point is a limit point of every set.
Limit points do not exist in T1 spaces.
A set has no limit points unless it is infinite.
If $x$ is a limit point of $A$, then every neighborhood of $x$ contains infinitely many points of $A$.
questions.explanation: In a T1 space, if $x$ is a limit point of $A$ then every neighborhood of $x$ meets $A$ in infinitely many points. If some neighborhood met $A$ in only finitely many points, removing those (which are closed) would yield a neighborhood of $x$ missing $A \setminus \{x\}$, contradicting that $x$ is a limit point.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: Among the topologies on $X = \{a, b\}$, which one is Hausdorff?
options: None of the topologies on a two-point set is Hausdorff.
questions.explanation: Only the discrete topology separates $a$ and $b$ by disjoint open singletons $\{a\}$ and $\{b\}$, so it is the only Hausdorff topology on a two-point set. The indiscrete topology is not even T0, and the Sierpinski-type topology is T0 but not T1 (hence not Hausdorff).
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: Take an infinite set $X$ with the cofinite topology. Decide its separation level by testing T1 (singletons closed) and T2 (disjoint neighborhoods).
questions.solution: A singleton $\{x\}$ has cofinite complement, which is open, so $\{x\}$ is closed and the space {{1}} T1. However, any two nonempty open sets meet, so distinct points cannot be separated by disjoint open sets, and the space {{2}} Hausdorff. The number of these two axioms (T1, T2) that this space satisfies is {{3}}.
questions.explanation: Cofinite topology: singletons are closed, so it is T1. Nonempty open sets always intersect, so it is not Hausdorff. Hence exactly $1$ of the two axioms holds.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: Classify the Sierpinski space $X = \{0, 1\}$ with topology $\{\emptyset, \{1\}, X\}$ by the lowest separation axioms.
questions.solution: The open set $\{1\}$ contains $1$ but not $0$, so the two points are distinguishable and the space {{1}} T0. Since the only open set containing $0$ is $X$, which also contains $1$, the singleton $\{0\}$ is not closed, so the space {{2}} T1. The number of open sets in this topology is {{3}}.
questions.explanation: The Sierpinski space is T0 (the open set $\{1\}$ distinguishes the points) but not T1 ($\{0\}$ is not closed). Its topology $\{\emptyset, \{1\}, X\}$ has $3$ open sets.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: Use the metric and finiteness facts. Let $X$ be a metric space and let $F$ be a finite Hausdorff space.
questions.solution: Every metric space separates distinct points by small disjoint balls, so $X$ {{1}} Hausdorff. A finite T1 space has every subset closed (a finite union of closed singletons), hence every subset open, so the finite Hausdorff space $F$ must be {{2}}. The number of the separation axioms T1 and T2 that an arbitrary metric space satisfies is {{3}}.
questions.explanation: Metric spaces are Hausdorff (disjoint balls of radius $d(x,y)/2$). A finite Hausdorff space is discrete. A metric space satisfies both T1 and T2, so the count is $2$.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: Apply the separation hierarchy and the Urysohn lemma hypothesis. Suppose a space is normal and T1 (that is, T4).
questions.solution: Under the usual convention, a T4 space (normal and T1) is also regular and T1, hence T3, and therefore also {{1}}, that is Hausdorff. The Urysohn lemma applies to normal spaces and separates two disjoint closed sets by a continuous function into the interval {{2}}. The number of the axioms among T0, T1, T2 that a T4 space satisfies is {{3}}.
questions.explanation: A T4 (normal + T1) space is T3, T2, T1, and T0. The Urysohn lemma yields a continuous $f : X \to [0,1]$ separating disjoint closed sets. A T4 space satisfies all of T0, T1, T2, that is $3$ of them.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology
